content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 1897 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1102 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 8, 1978

Mr. Stuart Honse
Executive Officer
California State Finance Agency
301 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Honse:

Thank you for your letter of October 28, 1978, suggesting
possible changes in the Fair Political Reform Act and Commission sign regulations regarding the duties of filing officers and
agency officials who receive Statements of Economic Interests
(Statements). Your suggestions have been discussed at the
highest staff level and although we greatly appreciate the
time and effort you contributed in considering our procedures
for processing Statements of Economic Interests, the staff
has decided not to recommend the changes you suggest.

As you know, the Political Reform Act designates the
Commission is the filing officer for heads of state agencies
and members of boards and commissions not under a department
of state government. (Government Code Section 3100(c)(6)).
One purpose of this provision is to assure the reliability
of such officials' statements through review by an independent agency. For the same reason, Statements of members of
the Fair Political Practices Commission are filed with and
reviewed by the Office of the Attorney General. Section
3102(c), which respect to other agency employees, the
agent itself is the filing officer and the head of the
agency can locally be held responsible for the appropriate
review of their Statements.

Though the Fair Political Practices Commission is the
filing officer for heads of agencies and members of independent boards and commissions, officers of the Commission believed
that a copy of every Statement filed by such officials should
be available for inspection at the official's agency as well
as at the Commission. And rather than having these officials
send a statement directly to the Commission with the Commission responsible for sending a copy back to the agency, the

Mr. Stuart House
Government & Ethics
Page Two

We requires these persons to file a Statement with their respective agencies holding them responsible for obtaining a copy and forwarding the original to the Commission. Just in terms of mail processing and record retention, the approach chosen by the drafters appears more efficient than the alternative you suggest. For example, in the case of a board comprised of five members, the board can collect Statements from all five members and send them to the Commission all at one time. With one mailing, therefore, all five Statements can be on file with both the agency and the Commission. If the alternative approach were instituted, each agency head and board or commission member probably would send a Statement to the Commission individually. The Commission would then have to process thousands of Statements filed from agencies all over the state, organize them by agency and forward copies of their respective agencies. Countless mailings by officials and many more by the Commission would be entailed.

The same rationale underlies the Commission's requirement that agencies provide blank Statements to their employees, including those that file with the Commission. In most cases, the agency official responsible for handling Statements has regular access to the head of his agency and members of his board or commission. If the Commission were required to provide the Statements directly, it would have to make individual mailings to each official. Therefore, in the case of a board or commission with five members, five mailings would be required to provide them with the Statements. These problems would be greatly compounded if agencies failed to provide the Commission with an up-to-date list of names and addresses of agency heads, board members and commissioners. An individual agency is simply not vulnerable to this problem with respect to its own employees.

As the official charged in your agency's Code with the responsibility of distributing Statements to designated employees, you are the logical person for filers to approach with questions regarding their Statements. You should feel free, of course, to consult with the Commission's Conflict of Interest Division staff and to refer designated employees to our Document Examiners for assistance in completing their Statements.

Mr. Stuart Honse
December 8, 1974
Page Three


Again, we greatly appreciate your consideration of the
procedures we are responsible to administer and your thought-
ful recommendations for change.

                                            Sincerely,

                                            [signature]
                                            Michael Bennett
                                            Executive Director

MB:cjb

CALIFORNIA HOUSING FINANCE AGENCY

October 28, 1978

Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

Several months ago we had a rather heated telephone conversation
in which I argued strenuously, but perhaps unconvincingly, that
the procedures with respect to the filing of conflict of interest
statements for board and commission members and agency chief
executive officers (exempt appointees) make no sense. In parti­
cular, I spoke to the procedures which require a designated de­
partmental filing officer to supply forms, instructions, and
notices to all of the department's exempt appointees. Further,
the filing officer is to receive the statements filed by those
respective officeholders, make a copy of same, and forward the
original to the FPPC who acts as "filing officer" for the pur­
poses of those officeholders and their filings. I indicated to
you that I felt such a procedure places the agency filing officer
in the untenable position of being partially held responsible for
the timing and correctness of the officeholder's statement, while
having no authority or responsibility to accept or reject the
appropriateness of that statement.

As you argued against my position, you made it clear that current
procedures are established by law and are not established at the
discretion of the FPPC. You suggested that I submit my concerns
along with any recommendations I might have, in a written form and
you would see that the FPPC management would review my recommenda­
tions and consider them for possible legislative recommendation on
behalf of the FPPC. This letter is in response to your recommenda­
tion.

I remain convinced that the current system is not only administra­
tively cumbersome, but additionally does not work in a positive
way to encourage and facilitate accurate, complete and timely
filing of officeholders' statements. I recommend in its stead,
legislative and regulatory changes which would install the follow­
ing type of procedures:

• FPPC would be designated as the filing officer for all exempt
appointees and officeholders. This means that the FPPC would
have administrative responsibility for notifying all office­

301 CAPITOL MALL                SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814           (916) 322-3991

Ted Prim, Chief                -2-                October 28, 1978


     holders of annual filing requirements, leaving office state-
     ments, etc.

     • Exempt appointees and officeholders would receive appropriate
       forms and instructions directly from the FPPC and would con-
       tact an appropriate FPPC officer for advice and counsel in
       the preparation of statements. Officeholders would submit
       originals of statements directly to FPPC who would in turn
       send informational copies (approved by FPPC) to the office-
       holder's department, for public access.

     • The filing officers within the various departments and
       agencies of State government would be required to notify the
       appropriate FPPC officer within five working days of changes
       in any exempt office within the agency or department. Such
       notice could be accomplished in a standardized form which
       would provide for the necessary detailed information regard-
       ing all of the possible changes affecting a particular designated
       position. (i.e., death, reappointment, appointment to other
       office, etc.)

     With the incorporation of the above recommendations (and I would
     be happy to work with you to refine them to a more detailed state)
     application of effort at the FPPC and the department or agency
     could be eliminated. Inasmuch as the FPPC already maintains a
     file on all exempt appointees and officeholders, and inasmuch as
     the FPPC functions as the filing officer for those appointees, it
     makes eminent sense then to link the administrative responsibility
     for filing notices and statement receipt with the body which ulti-
     mately has responsibility for adjudging the accuracy and correctness
     of an exempt appointee's filing. Such a system would further re-
     move the departmental filing officer from all conflicts associated
     with an exempt appointee(s') filing and problems arising thereto.

     Should you have any questions, please contact me at 2-3991.


                                        Stuart Honse
                                        Executive Officer
